coredata.dc:title: Strategy for establishing sensitive fluorescent sensor toward p-nitrophenol integrating magnetic molecularly imprinted materials and carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shuangying
Gao, Xiaozhong
Nie, Linchun
Bu, Lutong
Dong, Guangyu
Song, Denghao
Liu, Wenjing
Meng, Dejing
Geng, Xiaodie
Zhou, Qingxiang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a sensitive fluorescent sensor toward p-nitrophenol (4-NP) integrating magnetic molecularly imprinted materials and carbon dots (CDs) was proposed. Magnetic material and CDs derived from K3 [Fe(CN)6] and glucose were simultaneously obtained through simple one-step hydrothermal process. Introducing of molecularly imprinted materials based magnetic solid phase extraction (MSPE) endowed the constructed fluorescent sensor with higher sensitivity and selectivity. The significant factors affecting the sensitivity of the sensor toward 4-NP were optimized. Good linearity was obtained between fluorescent intensity of CDs and different concentration of 4-NP from 0.08 to 62.5 μg L−1. The sensitivity of constructed sensor was very low with detection limit of 0.02 μg L−1. Reliable applicability was also proved by the well-pleasing recoveries of 94.2–97.8% with different spiked concentrations of 4-NP in real environmental waters.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
K3[Fe(CN)6]
4-NP
Magnetic molecularly imprinted material
Fluorescent sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of CDs and magnetic molecularly imprinted materials
Characterization of the prepared CDs and MMIPs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detailed process of the fluorescent probe with CDs and MMIPs based MSPE for sensing 4-NP.

Characterization of CDs and MMIPs. (A) TEM morphologies of CDs; (B) Emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths; (C) and (D) Morphologies of the magnetic nanomaterial and MMIPs; (E) XPS spectra of CDs.

Investigation of the enrichment parameters. (A–F) Effect of adsorbent amount, eluents, adsorption time, eluent volume, elution time and sample pH. Initial conditions were set as follows: spiked concentration of 4-NP, 20 μg L−1; sample volume, 60 mL; adsorbent dosage, 35 mg; eluent, 6 mL mixture of methanol: AA (9:1); adsorption time, 50 min; elution time, 10 min. Each parameter was examined keeping other parameters constant, and the optimal value was utilized in the following procedure.

Optimization of the parameters in detection process. (A) Effect of synthesis time of CDs on measurement of 4-NP; (B) Effect of CDs concentration on measurement of 4-NP; (C) Effect of pH on measurement of 4-NP. Initial conditions: Synthesis time of CDs, 8 h; CDs concentration, 50 μg L−1; pH, pH 8.5.
(A) The UV–vis spectra of CDs, 4-NP and CDs + 4-NP; (B) FL emission spectrum of CDs and UV–vis adsorption spectrum of 4-NP; (C) FL lifetimes of CDs and CDs + 4-NP.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of 4-NP at different concentrations; (B) Linearity between the fluorescent intensity of CDs and the concentration of 4-NP from 0.08 to 62.5 μg L−1; (C) Selectivity of CDs; (D) Comparison of MMIPs and MNIPs toward 4-NP.
Comparison with other reported literatures for detection of 4-NP.

row.entry: PCDs and portable polyamide film-based sensor

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: MF-MIPs, magnetic fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymers; GQD, graphene quantum dots; YVO4:Eu3+, Eu3+-activated yttrium orthovanadate; PCDs, polymer carbon dots; NOCD, nitrogen doped oxidized carbon dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of real water samples with present sensor.

head.ce:title: Strategy for establishing sensitive fluorescent sensor toward p-nitrophenol integrating magnetic molecularly imprinted materials and carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing, 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical Engineering and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing, 102249, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a sensitive fluorescent sensor toward p-nitrophenol (4-NP) integrating magnetic molecularly imprinted materials and carbon dots (CDs) was proposed. Magnetic material and CDs derived from K3 [Fe(CN)6] and glucose were simultaneously obtained through simple one-step hydrothermal process. Introducing of molecularly imprinted materials based magnetic solid phase extraction (MSPE) endowed the constructed fluorescent sensor with higher sensitivity and selectivity. The significant factors affecting the sensitivity of the sensor toward 4-NP were optimized. Good linearity was obtained between fluorescent intensity of CDs and different concentration of 4-NP from 0.08 to 62.5 μg L−1. The sensitivity of constructed sensor was very low with detection limit of 0.02 μg L−1. Reliable applicability was also proved by the well-pleasing recoveries of 94.2–97.8% with different spiked concentrations of 4-NP in real environmental waters.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: 4-Nitrophenol (4-NP) was widely used as the synthetic intermediates in various fields such as pesticides, medicines, dyes, plastics and herbicides [1]. However, abundant use of 4-NP may cause environmental pollution. It can enter environment and spread long distances with wastewater, contaminating soil and ground water aquifers as the medium. Long term exposure to 4-NP may cause substantial negative health effects on human body [2,3]. Central nervous system of human body may be inhibited and destroyed, causing some serious discomfort feelings and irreparable damage, including breathlessness, confusion, and unconsciousness along with fatigue, dizziness, nausea, vomiting and diarrhea, etc. According to the damage toward human health, 4-NP has been listed as a toxic priority pollutant by the United States Environmental Protection Agency and its concentration has been restricted to below 10 ng L−1 in water [4,5]. Thus, establishing a rapid, reliable, efficient, sensitive method for detecting 4-NP is of great significance.

In recent years, more and more different strategies have been established for the detection of 4-NP, including high performance liquid chromatography, electrochemistry, fluorescence sensing [6–11], etc. Though all the methods are reliable for the analysis of 4-NP, most of them rely on the large-scale complex instruments, which not only need to spend more time for the analysis of target analytes in the detection process but also need to take more time and cost to maintain the instruments after use. Compared with the other methods, fluorescence analysis owns the outstanding characteristics of quick response, simple operation and high stability [12–15]. Fluorescent materials commonly used for fluorescent detection include metal clusters (MNCs), luminescent metal-organic frameworks (L-MOFs), semiconductor quantum dots, carbon dots (CDs) etc. Compared with the other fluorescent materials, CDs as a type of green and environment friendly nanomaterials have attracted many researchers' attention because of its unique optical features, including tunable photoluminescence, chemical stability, simple synthesis, low toxicity, good water solubility, cheap and abundant precursors [16], etc. Under the circumstance of environmental and global energy crisis as well as the requirement of sustainable development, it is essential to produce nanomaterials sustainably with environmentally friendly materials replacing hazardous chemicals. Thus, many CDs based fluorescence sensing platform were conducted for analysis of typical analytes in environmental samples. Peng et al. chose 3-aminophenylboronic acid as carbon and nitrogen sources for fabricating B,N-CDs, and designed a reliable and sensitive ratiometric sensor for HSO3 − determination based on B,N-CDs-Cr(VI) system. The principle was that the Fluorescent (FL) intensity of CDs at 450 nm obviously decreased in the presence of Cr(VI) contributed to inner filter effect (IFE), and the FL intensity significantly recovered with the addition of HSO3 − due to the reason that IFE effect was inhibited because of the reduction of Cr(VI). At the same time, FL intensity at 535 nm declined. FL intensity ratio (I450/I535) of B,N-CDs-Cr(VI) system changed with the increase of HSO3 − concentration. Satisfactory linearity was obtained in the range of 0–1000 μM. In addition, they also fabricated a paper test strip based on N,B-CDs for visual semiquantitative detection of HSO3 − [17]. Tang's group fabricated concentration-dependence multi-color fluorescent N,S-CDs for simultaneously monitoring rifampicin, morin and Al3+ with citric acid and thiourea as precursors. In the system, high concentration of N,S-CDs emitted green fluorescence which was employed for recognizing of rifampicin based on the mechanisms of photo-induced electron transfer and dynamic quenching, and low-concentration of N,S-CDs with blue fluorescence was utilized to construct fluorescence sensor for identifying morin based on the decrease of CDs' FL intensity caused by internal filtration effect and static quenching. According to the interaction between morin and Al3+, a “turn-on” fluorescent probe based on low concentration N,S-CDs-morin system was proposed for sequential detection of Al3+. LODs of rifampicin, morin and Al3+ were 51.2 nM, 56.6 nM and 45.8 nM. The sensor was also practically used for analyzing morin and Al3+ in actual samples including aluminum hydroxide tablets, urine and serum samples, which proved the potential appalment of the proposed sensor for disease diagnosis, water quality detection as well as biomedicine analysis [18].

However, pollutants always exist in environment at low levels, taking great difficulty to direct determination. Employing proper pretreatment technology to separate and enrich target analytes is of great necessity. In terms of sample pretreatment, solid phase extraction (SPE) is the most used technology due to its features of high efficiency and small amount of organic solvent usage [19]. However, there are still some drawbacks of SPE, such as poor selectivity of target analytes and great difficulty to reuse. Magnetic solid phase extraction (MSPE) is a new technology with similar mechanism of SPE that takes magnetic material as adsorbent to disperse in sample solution and then uses an external magnet to achieve rapid separation. MSPE can not only reduce the amount of toxic and harmful organic solvents but also simplify the sample pretreatment process avoiding filtration or centrifugation operations [20]. Molecularly imprinted polymer is a kind of stable synthetic polymer with selective molecular recognition sites. Magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers (MMIPs) combine with the advantages of molecularly imprinted polymer and magnetic material, and has higher selectivity in the identification and extraction of target analytes [21]. Molecularly imprinted polymers based MSPE can not only greatly improve the separation performance but also take great improvement to the enriching efficiency and selectivity. Bhogal et al. synthesized MMIPs by a surface imprinting method taking Fe3O4@SiO2 as the core material, diethyl phthalate and dicyclohexyl phthalate as dual dummy templates, which was effectively utilized for the selective identification and isolation for phthalates and exhibited great selectivity toward phthalates and good reusability [22]. Li et al. designed thermosensitive magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers (T-MMIPs) for selective enrichment of trace doxycycline, which exhibited excellent adsorption performance for enriching and rapid desorption of doxycycline by adjusting temperature in different solvents, and also showed excellent selectivity for doxycycline [23].

In present study, CDs and magnetic nanomaterial were prepared at the same time through one-pot hydrothermal process using K3 [Fe(CN)6] and glucose as the precursors. CDs were employed as the fluorescence probe for sensing 4-NP based on the quenching effect. Magnetic nanoparticles were used as magnetic core for synthesizing magnetic molecularly imprinting polymers through sol-gel method. Then the magnetic molecularly imprinting polymers were utilized as MSPE adsorbents for highly efficient extraction of 4-NP from sample solutions before CDs based fluorescence sensing. Key factors which may take negative or positive effect on enriching or sensing efficiency were studied and optimized. Detailed process of the constructed FL sensing probe integrating CDs and MMIP for sensing 4-NP was shown in Fig. 1 .

4-NP, 2-nitrophenol (2-NP), 3-nitrophenol (3-NP), K3 [Fe(CN)6], 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and NH3 •H2O with mass fraction of 25–28% were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All reagents are of analytical grade. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded by Escalab 250XI (Thermo, USA). Details of some reagents and apparatuses used in this work was as the same as that described in our previous work [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CDs and magnetic molecularly imprinted materials
ce:section.ce:para: CDs as well as magnetic material were simultaneously derived from K3 [Fe(CN)6] and glucose via one step hydrothermal process. Details were as follows: 0.5 g K3 [Fe(CN)6] and 0.2 g glucose were fully mixed with10 mL deionized water and then poured into 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. After holding at the temperature of 200 °C for 8 h, upper brown CDs solution and bottom layer precipitate were obtained. The precipitate showed good magnetic property. Finally, CDs solution was passed through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and then further purified with the aid of dialysis cellulose membrane (molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da) for 24 h. Meanwhile, the magnetic precipitate was collected for further use with the aid of external magnet after being washed with water and ethanol.

MMIPs were prepared via sol-gel method [25,26]. Briefly, 0.5 mL functional monomer APTES and 50 mg template molecule 4-NP were mixed in 50 mL ethanol and then stirred at 25 °C with 200 rpm. After stirring for 30 min, 0.5 mL cross-linking agent (TEOS) and 0.5 mL initiator (NH3⋅H2O) were added. Meanwhile, under the mechanical stirring, 0.5 g magnetic nanomaterial was added into the mixture and then kept stirring at 25 °C for 24 h. At last, the MMIPs were immersed in the solution of methanol and acetic acid (9:1, v/v) for several times to remove the templates. Simultaneously, MNIPs were prepared with the same process abovementioned but without addition of 4-NP.

Before direct fluorescent sensing of 4-NP, the introduction of MMIPs based MSPE can not only enrich 4-NP from water samples but also improve the sensitivity and selectivity of the method. The procedure was as follows: firstly, 35 mg MMIPs were added into 60 mL water sample with 5 μg L−1 4-NP. After shaking at 25 °C for 50 min, most of target analyte 4-NP was adsorbed on MMIPs. Then, in order to completely collect 4-NP, MMIPs were desorbed with the solution of methanol and acetic acid (9:1, v/v) for four cycles. Each cycle was involved in eluting with 1.5 mL solution of methanol and acetic acid (9:1, v/v) for 2 min. Then the collected eluent containing 4-NP was dried with N2. Finally, 1.2 mL CDs solution (0.5 mg mL−1) was added for fluorescence detection. Meanwhile, the same procedure was conducted without 4-NP. The fluorescence intensity was recorded. Excitation wavelength, emission wavelength and slit widths were set at 325 nm, 410 nm and 5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the prepared CDs and MMIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Morphologies of CDs and MMIPs were analyzed by transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fig. 2 A demonstrates that CDs exhibited nearly spherical morphology and good dispersion. Fluorescent performance of the prepared CDs was also studied. It was apparent that the FL emission intensity of the CDs firstly increased and then decreased with the change of the excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2B). In the range of 310–325 nm, the FL intensity of CDs increased accompanying with the rise of excitation wavelength and the emission wavelength did not change with the change of excitation wavelength. Over range of 325–340 nm, FL intensity of CDs gradually decreased and the emission wavelength occurred slightly red shift. Except fluorescence performance, stability and resistance to photobleaching of CDs were also studied. As shown in Fig. S1, FL intensity of CDs had no obvious change in 90 days, indicating the prepared CDs possessed the excellent stability. In order to observe the resistance to photobleaching of CDs, CDs solution was exposure to 365 nm UV lamp and 420 nm Xe lamp. According to Fig. S2, the FL intensity of CDs didn't change in 45 min. All the results demonstrated that the prepared CDs had excellent stability and resistance to photobleaching. Fig. 2C and D shows the TEM images of the magnetic nanomaterials and MMIPs. Obviously, a layer of polymer deposited on the magnetic nanomaterials could be seen and there was a certain amount of Si element emerged in the mapping images, which confirmed the successful preparation of MMIPs. XPS analysis of CDs were depicted in Fig. 2E, and which confirmed that C, N, O, K elements were present in CDs. Peaks located at 284.5, 286.5 and 287.9 eV can be assigned to C–C/CC, C–O/C–N and CO [27], respectively, which indicated there were abundant surface functional groups on the as-prepared CDs.

In order to guarantee excellent performance of the proposed method, the crucial factors affecting the MSPE procedure and FL sensing such as adsorbent amount, adsorption time, eluent, eluent volume, elution time, sample pH, synthesis time of CDs, CDs concentration and sample pH were optimized. All the experiments were repeated three times (n = 3).

In MSPE procedure, adsorbent is the major factor in enriching the target analytes from various matrix [23]. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the adsorption efficiency of 4-NP increased following the increase of adsorbent amount from 5 to 35 mg. When adsorbent amount continually increased, the adsorption efficiency increased very little, indicating that 35 mg adsorbent was enough for enriching 4-NP from the water samples. After completing adsorption process, it is of great significance to choose a proper eluent for separating the target analytes from the adsorbents. In this study, the influence of different eluents on recovery of 4-NP was investigated and the results were demonstrated in Fig. 3B. It was obviously that the elution efficiency of the eluent followed the order of mixed solution of methanol and acetic acid (9:1, v/v), methanol, ethanol, acetone and acetonitrile. The reason may be due to that 4-007ANP is a polar compound, and easily dissolved in the polar solvent, and the polarities of the involved eluents followed the order of methanol and acetic acid (9:1, v/v), methanol, ethanol, acetone and acetonitrile. Hence the mixture of methanol: acetic acid (AA) (9:1, v/v) resulted in the highest elution efficiency as seen from Fig. 3B. Adsorption time is another crucial for obtaining good enrichment performance. The influence of adsorption time on the extraction efficiency was also studied over the range of 5–60 min, and it could be observed that the extraction efficiency rose up along the time prolonging to 50 min, and the peak value was achieved at 50 min, which meant that the adsorption almost reached equilibrium as depicted from Fig. 3C. Meanwhile, the elution process is also a key step that should be considered, and the option of eluent volume and elution time takes great place in the procedure of elution [28]. Less time and volume may be not enough for eluting the targets from MMIPs, resulting in incomplete elution and further low sensitivity, which can not only take difficulty to the reuse of MMIPs but also impose restrictions on obtaining higher recovery. However, excess time and eluent volume may decline the merits of the proposed method, waste solvent and time, and increase the cost of the method development. The eluent volume and elution time were investigated in the range of 1.5–9 mL and 2–12 min, respectively. As demonstrated in Fig. 3D and E, it was apparent that the recoveries of 4-NP increased with the increase of the eluent volume and elution time with the eluent volume changed from 1.5 to 6 mL, and the elution time changed from 2 to 8 min, further no remarkable change occurred, which demonstrated that satisfactory eluting performance could be obtained with the elution volume and elution time keeping at 6 mL and 8 min, respectively. More time or solvent was not favorable for enhancing eluting performance. In MSPE system, the effect of pH cannot be ignored [29]. MMIPs would be corroded in strong acidic condition, resulting in poor adsorption performance, which explained the lower recoveries of 4-NP at pH 3 and pH 5 in Fig. 3F. Under alkaline condition, the recovery of 4-NP also decreased with the rise of pH value. This may be imputed to the fact that the pKa of 4-NP is 7 while pKa = pH + log (undissociated concentration of analyte)/(ionized concentration), and 4-NP existed in dissociated forms, which cannot be easily adsorbed by MMIPs [30].

In the process of establishing a novel detection method, enriching analytes from sample solution plays an important role in increase of the sensitivity of the developing method, and the optimization of detection parameters is also of great importance. Fig. 4 A–C exhibits the effects of synthesis time of CDs, CDs concentration and final sample solution pH on measurement of 4-NP. Carbonization degree of CDs varied with the different time of hydrothermal reaction, and resulted in great fluorescent difference of the obtained CDs and different quenching effect which was represented as ΔF (F0–F) (F0 represents FL intensity of CDs, while F represents FL intensity of CDs + 4-NP). Fig. 4A demonstrates that the change of fluorescent intensity firstly increased and then declined with prolonging the hydrothermal reaction time ranging from 4 to 16 h, and the maximum value was achieved at 8 h. Thus, hydrothermal reaction time was set as 8 h. The concentration of CDs is of enormous importance in the developing method. Fig. 4B indicates that the ΔF gradually raised with the rise of CDs concentration ranging from 20 to 50 μg L−1, when the concentration of CDs continuously increased, ΔF declined slightly. The phenomenon can be explained by the fact that more amounts of CDs provide more binding sites for target analytes, and when CDs concentration was at high level, self-quenching effect may occur due to the emergence of the aggregated state resulting in poor analysis of 4-NP. Solution pH always takes great effect on the fluorescent performance of CDs. Hence the influence of pH on measurement of 4-NP was optimized in the range of pH4-9. As exhibited in Fig. 4C, ΔF was very small in acidic environment and rapidly increased with the increase of pH value from pH 6.5 to pH 8.5. When pH was at 8.5, ΔF reached the highest. So, pH 8.5 was employed in the further detection process.

For exploring the sensing mechanism of quenching effect occurred in this work, a series of experiments were conducted. Fig. 5 A depicts the UV–vis spectra of 4-NP, CDs, and mixed solution of CDs + 4-NP. It could be seen that there were not new peaks appearing in the UV–vis spectrum of CDs + 4-NP compared with the spectra of 4-NP and CDs, indicating that there were no new substances forming in the process of sensing procedure. Generally, non-fluorescent ground-state complex may be formed in the system of static quenching effect (SQE), which always causes the change of UV–vis absorption peaks. According the results, the possibility of the quenching effect attributing to SQE was excluded. Except SQE, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE) or photoinduced electron transfer (PET) may explain the quenching effect occurred in this work [31]. For deeply understanding the sensing mechanism, the FL emission spectrum of CDs was compared with the UV–vis spectrum of 4-NP (Fig. 5B). It could be seen that FL emission spectrum of CDs coincided with the UV–vis spectrum of 4-NP, which proved that the sensing mechanism occurred in this work may be ascribed to FRET or IFE. To determine which mechanism would explain the quenching effect much better, FL lifetimes of CDs and the mixed solution of CDs + 4-NP were measured. Fig. 5C exhibits that FL lifetime of CDs (3.87 ns) didn't almost change comparing with that of the mixed solution (3.83 ns). Basing on the fact that fluorescence lifetimes will change largely in the principle of FRET, the quenching effect occurred in this work could not be ascribed to FRET. According to all the results and analysis mentioned above, the sensing mechanism of quenching effect was attributed to IFE.

MMIPs based MSPE was employed for separating and enriching 4-NP before FL sensing with CDs. The participation of MMIPs based MSPE brought forth some benefits, the most of all was great improvement of sensitivity and selectivity toward 4-NP. As exhibited in Fig. 6 A and 6B, the method exhibited good linearity in two ranges of 0.08–1.67 μg L−1 and 1.67–62.5 μg L−1. The limit of detection (LOD) was explored by recording the FL intensity of carbon dots with blank samples after MSPE pretreatment for 15 times. According to the algorithm formula of 3σ/slope (where σ represents the standard deviation of blank signals (n = 15)), The LOD of 4-NP was 0.02 μg L−1. Fig. 6C exhibits the good selectivity of CDs toward 4-NP. Only with addition of 4-NP, obvious decrease of FL intensity of CDs could be seen, but 2-nitrophenol (2-NP) and 3-nitrophenol (3-NP) resulted in very small decrease of FL intensity of CDs. Fig. 6D depicts the different adsorption efficiencies of MMIPs and MNIPs. It could be seen that the recovery of MMIPs was much higher than that of MNIPs, indicating the successful synthesis of MMIPs and the excellent selectively adsorption performance of MMIPs toward 4-NP. There were also some other sensing platforms being proposed and reported for analysis of 4-NP. For example, Xiong et al. designed and prepared a fluorescent functionalized chitosan sensor for sensing of 4-NP with chitosan as matrix, naphthalimide as fluorophore and phenyl isothiocyanate as the modification reagent. They found that introduction of electron-rich CS bond into fluorescent functionalized chitosan sensor made it easy to be combined with the electron-deficient nitrophenol and effectively enhance quenching efficiency. The fluorescent functionalized chitosan sensor showed high sensitive and selective recognition ability to 4-NP with LOD of 0.9 μM [32]. Goswami et al. fabricated a highly luminous reduced graphene oxide nano-probe (Asp-rGO) by reducing and functionalizing graphene oxide with aspartic acid via hydrothermal method, and Asp-rGO possessed good water dispersibility. Asp-rGO exhibited excellent fluorescence response to 4-NP due to IFE, and constructed sensor demonstrated high stability, good selectivity and high sensitivity with LOD of 11.465 nM in aqueous solution [33]. Though the sensing platforms mentioned above exhibited excellent sensing performance for 4-NP analysis, CDs based fluorescent sensor proposed in this work had not only the advantages of better FL performance, high selectivity and sensitivity, but also owned the outstanding features of easy synthesis, environmental friendliness and low cost. The comparison of present work with other FL sensors for detection of 4-NP also could be seen in Table 1 , it exhibited that the combination of MMIP based MSPE and CDs based FL sensor proposed in this work possessed lower LOD. In addition, employment of MSPE could obviously improve the sensitivity of the FL sensor compared with the single CDs based FL sensor [34–39]. What's more, the reusability of the MMIPs was also assessed, and the results were provided in the supporting information. For assessing the reusability of the MMIPs, the same MMIPs were used in the repeated MSPE procedure, and the recoveries were measured. According to the data presented in Fig. S3, when the MMIPs were used six times, the extraction efficiency for enriching 4-NP only decreased slightly. The results indicated that the prepared MMIPs nanomaterial owned better reusability.

To further validate the practicality of the CDs based FL sensor combined with MSPE proposed in this work, three water samples were collected and analyzed. All the samples including reclaimed water, Kunming Lake water and Flower Sea Lake water were obtained from Changping, Beijing. Before analysis, the samples were filtered through 0.22 μm filter membrane without other treatment. As exhibited in Table 2 , the spiked recoveries of 4-NP were in the range of 96.4–98.8% with RSDs over range of 1.6–3.1% at different spiked levels. In addition, selectivity of fluorescent sensor in complex environments were also assessed. Different heavy metal ion with concentration of 10 μM was added into reclaimed water sample spiked with 4-NP at 0.05 μM. The experimental process was performed as mentioned above. As shown in Fig. S4, addition of heavy metal ions didn't take obvious negative effect on the sensing of 4-NP. All the results demonstrated that the CDs based sensing platform combined with MMIPs based MSPE was reliable for the detection of 4-NP in real samples, which created an effective alternative way for the analysis of 4-NP, which was of great significance in monitoring environmental pollutants.

In this work, magnetic nanoparticles and CDs were simultaneously synthesized by one-step hydrothermal process using K3 [Fe(CN)6] and glucose as iron and carbon sources, which saved a certain cost and time for method development, and simplified the preparation process of sensing sensor. The magnetic molecular imprinting materials demonstrated excellently selective adsorption of 4-NP. The established magnetic solid phase extraction exhibited good enrichment of 4-NP, and which was successfully a good step in the developed fluorescent sensor for selectively and sensitively monitoring of 4-NP in environmental water samples. The experimental results demonstrated that the developed sensor in this work greatly improved the selectivity and sensitivity for detecting 4-NP, and demonstrated good merits of simplicity, rapidness, low cost and easy for use, and may be optioned as an effective alternative in the analysis of 4-NP.

Shuangying Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiaozhong Gao: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Linchun Nie: Validation, Software, Formal analysis. Lutong Bu: Software, Resources, Data curation. Guangyu Dong: Resources, Formal analysis. Denghao Song: Investigation, Formal analysis. Wenjing Liu: Validation, Software. Dejing Meng: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiaodie Geng: Resources, Formal analysis. Qingxiang Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2024.125749.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bicarbonate-enhanced iron-based Prussian blue analogs catalyze the Fenton-like degradation of p-nitrophenol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yang, Y. Gu, H. Lin, B. Jie, Z. Zheng, X. Zhang, Bicarbonate-enhanced iron-based Prussian blue analogs catalyze the Fenton-like degradation of p-nitrophenol, J. Colloid Interf. Sci. 608 (2022) 2884-2895.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced reduction of p-nitrophenol by zerovalent iron modified with carbon quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Fan, X. Zhang, H. Deng, X. Guan, Enhanced reduction of p-nitrophenol by zerovalent iron modified with carbon quantum dots, Appl. Catal. B-Environ. 285 (2021) 119829.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coupling adsorption and degradation in p-nitrophenol removal by biochars

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot preparations of cyclodextrin polymer-entrapped nano zero-valent iron for the removal of p-nitrophenol in water
